title: Lower bounds on the size of semidefinite programming relaxations
speaker: David Steurer
abstract: We introduce a method for proving lower bounds on the efficacy of semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations for combinatorial problems. In particular, we show that the cut, TSP, and stable set polytopes on $n$-vertex graphs are not the linear image of the feasible region of any SDP (i.e., any spectrahedron) of dimension less than $2^{n^{\delta}}$, for some constant $\delta > 0$. This result yields the first super-polynomial lower bounds on the semidefinite extension complexity of any explicit family of polytopes.

proof_stages.label: Lower-bound goal and SOS-universality strategy
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the aim of proving unconditional super-polynomial lower bounds for LP/SDP relaxation algorithms by showing that the concrete sum-of-squares/Lasserre hierarchy is optimal in the general Yannakakis-style model and then transferring known SOS integrality gaps.

proof_stages.label: Mathematical-programming relaxation landscape
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates the model by comparing linear, spectral, and semidefinite relaxations, emphasizing that SDP methods unify LP and spectral methods while remaining structured enough to hope for unconditional lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Yannakakis lift model via MaxCut
proof_stages.summary: MaxCut is formulated as quadratic optimization on the hypercube and as linear optimization over the cut polytope, leading to the definition of small LP formulations as higher-dimensional polytopes projecting to the cut polytope, with a discussion of size, auxiliary variables, exactness, and approximation.

proof_stages.label: From LP lower bounds to SDP formulation lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: Known Fiorini-type LP lower bounds are recalled, the SDP extension of the lift model is described by replacing polytopes with spectrahedra, and the new theorem is stated as super-polynomial or exponential SDP lower bounds matching the guarantees of sum-of-squares and current CSP hardness results.

proof_stages.label: Certificate and pseudo-distribution viewpoint
proof_stages.summary: The geometric formulation is replaced by a certificate view: approximation algorithms certify near-optimal upper bounds, degree-d SOS certificates express nonnegativity as sums of squares of low-degree functions, and failure of such certificates yields separating hyperplanes interpretable as pseudo-distributions with PSD moment matrices.

proof_stages.label: General SDP certificates from a matrix-valued slack function
proof_stages.summary: An arbitrary SDP relaxation is encoded by a PSD matrix-valued function q(x), obtained as the slack of the spectrahedral constraint at true solutions, and SDP duality shows that all certificates available to the relaxation are generated as traces against PSD matrices.

proof_stages.label: Low-degree square-root criterion and approximation target
proof_stages.summary: The key reduction is that if the matrix square root of q has low polynomial degree then its certificates are SOS-certifiable, so it suffices to replace any small SDP q by a q′ preserving correlations with low-degree tests while making sqrt(q′) low-degree.

proof_stages.label: Maximum-entropy approximation and Taylor degree control
proof_stages.summary: The approximating q′ is chosen as the maximum von Neumann entropy solution matching the test correlations, yielding the closed form q′=exp(TF); because sqrt(q′)=exp(TF/2), Taylor truncation bounds its degree in terms of T and deg(F), and a later bootstrapping step removes the remaining logarithmic loss.

examples.name: L1 unit ball extended formulation
examples.note: The L1 unit ball, which has exponentially many direct facet inequalities but a linear-size lift using auxiliary absolute-value variables, illustrates why extension formulations can be exponentially smaller than direct descriptions.
examples.engagement_reason: The L1 unit ball is explicitly analyzed: the speaker counts its exponential direct inequalities and constructs a smaller extended formulation using auxiliary variables for absolute values.
